focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

Mud took Lale to the ankle the moment she stepped onto Halcote's quay, and the culverts under the planks answered with a thick wet breath. The town no longer smelled the way she remembered. Flour and brick dust were still in it somewhere, but the flood had worked both into a wet paste that coated the wharf planks and climbed the walls in pale streaks. The loading basin itself was fuller than it should have been. Water had backed up through the grain drains and the old quarry run, and every culvert mouth along the quay made the same thick breathing sound, as if the town had grown lungs in its foundations.

Halcote's people stood back from the edge. They did not want to seem frightened, which only made the fear look expensive. Men in mill aprons held their own elbows. Women with market shawls tucked their hands under their arms and stared at the ferry as if rot might jump the gap. A mule cart had been left crooked in the lane with one wheel sunk to the axle in mud. The driver had apparently gone off in a hurry and forgotten the carrots still tied in a bunch behind the seat. One of them had slipped loose and floated in the gutter water like a severed orange finger.

Lale saw Oren Pike before he saw her. He had his waxed hat on, though the brim had gone soft in the rain, and he was biting a clove the way he always did when he wanted to smell wealthier than the room allowed. He kept one hand under his ledger satchel, protecting it from the weather with more tenderness than most men here gave their children. Two years ago he had haggled her down over shroud cloth and then praised her accounts in the same breath, a neat inland trick that had almost made him tolerable. Now he was looking at the five of them as if Reedmouth had sent a disease upriver wearing a fami...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...in the only ledger he trusted.

By full dark the ropehouse fire had burned down to steaming black ribs. The yard stank of wet ash and cooked hemp. The opened fourth cut pulled clean now between the drowned posts, its surface flattened to a hard dark slick. Char from Riva's deck bumped a piling once, turned, and slipped under.

They loaded the funeral ferry in silence with one coil, two poles, blankets, the ledger, food, and medicine. The little that was left of the mission fit too easily.

When Iven stepped into the narrow hull at last, it rocked under him with the intimate balance of a craft built for the dead. Enver stood on the dock with his leg bound rough, one soot-black hand on a mooring post. Riva took the stern. Daro and Lale settled Nami low in the middle, her bandaged hand tucked against her belly. No one looked bigger in that boat than grief.

Iven set the salvaged pole against the gunwale and glanced once toward the place where the Bitter Thread had gone down. He could not see the wreck now, only rain stippling the cut and the occasional drift of black char moving seaward.

Nobody on the dock looked straight at him. They watched the ferry, the cut, Enver's bad leg, anything but his face. Under Iven's feet the little hull rocked coffin-narrow. His scorched eyebrow prickled, his palms throbbed inside the gloves, and Riva's boat lay somewhere below the black water because he had put his hands on a man before he put them on the work.

Riva shoved them off.

The funeral ferry slipped into the opened water like a blade entering old scar tissue. Behind them Coil Reach smoked in the rain. Ahead lay less boat, less gear, less room for performance, and no decent place yet to put Nami down out of the weather. Iven put both blistered hands on the pole anyway and pushed.
